original_abstract: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are currently the most commonly used non-viral gene delivery system. Their physiochemical attributes, encompassing size, charge and surface modifications, significantly affect their behaviors both in vivo and in vitro. Nevertheless, the effects of these properties on the transfection and distribution of LNPs after intramuscular injection remain elusive. In this study, LNPs with varying sizes, lipid-based charges and PEGylated lipids were formulated to study their transfection and in vivo distribution. Luciferase mRNA (mLuc) was entraped in LNPs as a model nucleic acid molecule. Results indicated that smaller-sized LNPs and those with neutral potential presented superior transfection efficiency after intramuscular injection. Surprisingly, the sizes and charges did not exert a notable influence on the in vivo distribution of the LNPs. Furthermore, PEGylated lipids with shorter acyl chains contributed to enhanced transfection efficiency due to their superior cellular uptake and lysosomal escape capabilities. Notably, the mechanisms underlying cellular uptake differed among LNPs containing various types of PEGylated lipids, which was primarily attributed to the length of their acyl chain. Together, these insights underscore the pivotal role of nanoparticle characteristics and PEGylated lipids in the intramuscular route. This study not only fills crucial knowledge gaps but also provides significant directions for the effective delivery of mRNA via LNPs. Role of size, surface charge, and PEGylated lipids of lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) on intramuscular delivery of mRNA Weiwen Kong1, Yuning Wei1, Zirong Dong1, Wenjuan Liu1, Jiaxin Zhao1, Yan Huang2,3, Jinlong Yang1, Wei Wu1, Haisheng He1* and Jianping Qi1* Page 2 of 16 Kong et al. Journal of Nanobiotechnology (2024) 22:553
humanized_abstract: Background Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are the most widely used non-viral vector for gene delivery. Physical characteristics such as size, surface charge and PEGylation of LNPs could play important roles in their in vivo and in vitro behavior. However, it is still unclear that which characteristic of LNPs could affect the transfection efficiency and distribution of LNPs after intramuscular administration, and how PEGylated lipids affect mRNA delivery. Methods Herein, a range of mRNA-LNPs formulations were prepared by adjusting the diameter, charge of lipid components and the types and content of PEGylated lipids. The effects of these variables on the transfection efficacy of the mRNA in vivo following intramuscular administration were assessed using a luciferase encoding mRNA, named as mLuc. The distribution of LNPs in vivo was also evaluated by ICP-MS, as well as the influence of PEGylated lipids on the internalization and escape behavior from the lysosome of mRNA-LNPs. Conclusion This study revealed that smaller diameter and neutral surface charge led to higher transfection efficiency of mRNA-LNPs following intramuscular administration. Moreover, varying PEGylated lipids with different acyl chains showed different cellular uptake behaviors and transfection efficacy in vivo. These results suggest that size, charge of LNPs and the structure of PEGylated lipids are important for intramuscular mRNA delivery. The findings of this study can significantly facilitate our understanding of the design of LNPs for mRNA therapy.
